---
title: "2026 Pharmaverse Hackathons"
description: "The official 2026 pharmaverse hackathon series — and how to prepare"
---

The pharmaverse council is running its first official hackathon series in 2026, explicitly designed to welcome first-time contributors. Resources are provided regardless of experience level — no prior open-source contribution experience is assumed.

::: {.callout-note}
## Source
Details on this page are drawn from the pharmaverse OSS Pillar blog post: [pharmaverse.github.io/blog](https://pharmaverse.github.io/blog/posts/2026-03-06-pharmaverse-pillar/pharmaverse-pillar-oss.html)
:::

---

## How Each Hackathon Works

Each event follows a structured three-week format:

```{=html}
<div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(240px, 1fr)); gap: 1rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;">

  <div style="background: #f4f8fb; border-top: 4px solid #2C6E8A; border-radius: 6px; padding: 1.2rem;">
    <div style="font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 700; color: #2C6E8A; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em; margin-bottom: 0.4rem;">Week 1</div>
    <div style="font-weight: 700; font-size: 1rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; font-family: 'Bitter', Georgia, serif;">Preparation</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.88rem; color: #4a5568; line-height: 1.55;">A 60-minute kick-off introduces the package, a curated set of issues, and contributor guidelines. No prior knowledge expected.</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: #f4f8fb; border-top: 4px solid #3FA88B; border-radius: 6px; padding: 1.2rem;">
    <div style="font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 700; color: #3FA88B; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em; margin-bottom: 0.4rem;">Week 2</div>
    <div style="font-weight: 700; font-size: 1rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; font-family: 'Bitter', Georgia, serif;">Execution</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.88rem; color: #4a5568; line-height: 1.55;">A 3–4 hour hands-on hackathon event. Work on real issues with OS mentors and the package team present throughout.</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: #f4f8fb; border-top: 4px solid #E8832A; border-radius: 6px; padding: 1.2rem;">
    <div style="font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 700; color: #E8832A; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em; margin-bottom: 0.4rem;">Week 3</div>
    <div style="font-weight: 700; font-size: 1rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; font-family: 'Bitter', Georgia, serif;">Wrap-Up</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.88rem; color: #4a5568; line-height: 1.55;">Pull request review with feedback from the package team. A final office hour session closes out and celebrates contributions.</div>
  </div>

</div>
```

This format is well-suited for a first contribution: scoped, supported, and explicitly welcoming of newcomers.

---

## 2026 Hackathon Calendar

```{=html}
<div class="hackathon-table">
<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Package</th>
      <th>Target Date</th>
      <th>Timezone</th>
      <th>Notes</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><code>{teal}</code> @ R/Medicine</td>
      <td>April 23</td>
      <td>PST</td>
      <td><span class="badge-past">Past</span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>{xportr}</code></td>
      <td>May 21</td>
      <td>EDT</td>
      <td><span class="badge-past">Past</span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>{aNCA}</code></td>
      <td>July 16</td>
      <td>CET</td>
      <td>NCA-focused; PK background helpful</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>{autoslider}</code> @ R/Pharma</td>
      <td>Week of Sept 28</td>
      <td>CST</td>
      <td>Reporting automation</td>
    </tr>
    <tr class="highlight-row">
      <td><code>{gtsummary}</code> &amp; <code>{cards/x}</code> @ R/Pharma</td>
      <td>Week of Sept 28</td>
      <td>PST</td>
      <td><span class="badge-recommended">Recommended</span> Good entry point for SAS programmers</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><code>{logrx}</code></td>
      <td>Early November</td>
      <td>EST</td>
      <td>Logging and compliance focus</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>
```

---

## Recommended Target: `{gtsummary}` & `{cards/x}`

For SAS programmers new to R, the **`{gtsummary}` and `{cards/x}` hackathon** (late September, R/Pharma conference week) is the strongest entry point. Here's why:

- **Approachable package:** `{gtsummary}` produces summary tables — directly analogous to `PROC TABULATE` / `PROC REPORT` output work you already do
- **Great documentation:** Among the best-documented packages in the ecosystem; getting started is genuinely easy
- **Enough runway:** Late September gives several months to build R comfort before the event
- **Supported entry:** The R/Pharma conference context means extra visibility, community energy, and mentoring support

::: {.callout-tip}
## If you have PK programming experience
Consider the `{aNCA}` hackathon in July (CET timezone). It focuses on non-compartmental analysis — a domain where clinical SAS programmers with PK backgrounds can contribute meaningfully from the start.
:::

---

## Preparing for Your First Hackathon

You don't need to be a proficient R programmer to contribute. Here's what actually helps:

**Before the kick-off (Week 1):**

- Have a working R environment and GitHub account (Steps 1–2)
- Have browsed the package documentation site at least once
- Have read a few open issues on the package GitHub repo

**At the kick-off:**

- Show up with questions, not answers — the kick-off is designed to orient you
- The package team will present curated issues rated by difficulty
- Choose an issue that matches your comfort level; "Good First Issue" labels are your friend

**During the hackathon (Week 2):**

- Mentors are present throughout — don't hesitate to ask for help
- A submitted pull request with honest effort is a success, regardless of whether it merges
- Your SAS background gives you clinical context that pure R programmers may not have — that's valuable

::: {.callout-note}
## First-time contributor resources
The pharmaverse blog has a dedicated post with tips for first-time contributors:  
[Tips for First-Time Contributors →](https://pharmaverse.github.io/blog/posts/2024-03-11_tips_for__first_/tips_for__first__time__contributors.html)
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## What's next after your first PR?

Once you've got a merged (or even just submitted) hackathon PR behind you, consider [R Consortium Pilot 6](pilot6.qmd) as a next step — real ADaM and TLF submission work in R, at your own pace. It's a bigger commitment than a hackathon (it uses DVC alongside Git, for one), so it's worth reading [Beyond the Hackathons](pilot6.qmd) before diving in.
:::
